name: Big Five Short-Form Instruments (BFI-2, TIPI, BFI-S)
core_claim: All three instruments measure the same five orthogonal personality domains of the Five-Factor Model: Openness/Open-Mindedness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Negative Emotionality (a.k.a. Neuroticism / reverse-scored Emotional Stability). They differ only in length and provenance: the BFI-2-XS (12 items, Soto & John 2017) and BFI-2-S (30 items) are abbreviations of the 60-item BFI-2; the TIPI (10 items, 2 per domain; Gosling, Rentfrow & Swann 2003) is an explicitly minimalist scale; the BFI-S (15 items, 3 per domain; Gerlitz & Schupp 2005, used in the German SOEP panel) sits between them. The theoretical link to media preference is that stable trait dispositions shape the *kinds of stimulation, meaning, and emotional experience a person seeks from entertainment*. The strongest and most consistent bridge is Openness, which predicts appetite for complex, novel, aesthetically unfamiliar, and ambiguous content; Extraversion relates to socially energetic/upbeat content; Neuroticism/Negative Emotionality relates to mood-regulation and intense/dark content; the FFM thus provides a cold-start prior over content style before any viewing history exists.
predictive_validity_for_media: Effect sizes for personality-to-media-preference links are real but small, which is the central caveat. Openness is the standout: it predicts preference for complex/intellectually demanding film (documentaries, art-house, independent film), unconventional books, and 'cerebral'/'aesthetic'/'dark' entertainment dimensions. Rentfrow, Goldberg & Zilca (2011, 'Listening, Watching, and Reading', Journal of Personality, N>3,000) derived a clean 5-factor structure of entertainment preferences — Communal, Aesthetic, Dark, Thrilling, Cerebral — and showed each maps onto Big Five traits, with Openness driving Aesthetic and Cerebral. This is the canonical media-preference paper and is the best-evidenced cross-medium link (it spans music, film, books). However, meta-analytic and review work consistently finds weighted-average correlations between single Big Five traits and specific genre preferences are very small: in pooled analyses most coefficients sit near zero and only a minority exceed |r|=0.1; Openness shows the largest and most reliable associations (commonly r ~0.2-0.3 for complex/artistic content, dropping toward ~0.1 elsewhere). Distinguishing the four prediction targets: (a) GENRE preference — modest, Openness strongest; (b) THEMATIC preference — under-studied directly but the Rentfrow factor structure (Dark/Cerebral/Aesthetic) is essentially thematic and shows clearer structure than raw genre; (c) NARRATIVE STYLE — Openness predicts tolerance for nonlinearity/ambiguity/complexity, the cleanest single mapping; (d) COMPLETION RATE — no strong direct evidence located linking short-form Big Five to binge/completion behaviour; treat as unsupported. [uncertain] Net: personality is a weak-but-nonzero prior, far more useful as a cold-start seed than as a primary ranking signal.
instrument_design: BFI-2-XS: 15 items total per the canonical Soto & John (2017) short-forms paper (3 per domain) — note the task framing of '12 items' likely conflates with the 12-item domain scales of the BFI-2-S; the published extra-short form is 15 items, ~2-3 min completion. [uncertain] TIPI: 10 items (2 per domain, one positively + one negatively keyed), 7-point Likert, each item presents two trait adjectives (e.g. 'Extraverted, enthusiastic'), ~1 minute completion — the lowest-friction validated FFM measure available. BFI-S: 15 items (3 per domain), 7-point agreement scale, ~2 min completion, validated against NEO-PI-R with acceptable convergent/discriminant validity and 18-month stability (weak spot: Agreeableness). All use self-report Likert agreement. MINIMUM VIABLE under-2-min / 6-10 item version: the TIPI (10 items) is the off-the-shelf fit and is the only widely validated FFM instrument that cleanly meets the 6-10 item, <2-min constraint. A 5-item single-per-domain reduction is NOT recommended — single-item-per-trait reliability collapses; TIPI's two-item design is already the floor of acceptable validity. If even 10 items is too long, drop domains rather than items per domain (e.g. measure only Openness + Extraversion with 4 items), since per-domain reliability matters more than breadth for a recommendation prior.
cold_start_applicability: Directly applicable and one of the few signals available with literally zero viewing history — this is the instrument category's main strength for HelpME2C. It yields a usable prior over content *style* (complexity, darkness, intensity, social energy) before the first interaction. Signal quality is modest (small effect sizes, see predictive_validity) but non-zero and, crucially, available at t=0. It is inherently cross-medium: the same Openness score informs TV, film, and anime preference simultaneously, which suits HelpME2C's cross-medium taxonomy — Rentfrow 2011 explicitly demonstrated the trait structure holds across music/film/books, so predicting TV/anime style from personality alone is theoretically supported. Best used as a soft seed that biases the initial candidate set and decays rapidly as real interaction data arrives. Do not treat it as a strong discriminator between specific titles.
group_compatibility_use: This is where short-form Big Five is most directly useful for HelpME2C's two-person core. Two profiles can be combined meaningfully because the five domains are continuous and on a common scale. Standard group-recommender aggregation strategies apply: (1) AVERAGE — mean of the two trait vectors, maximises mean satisfaction, good default; (2) LEAST-MISERY / 'average without misery' — recommend toward the more constrained member to avoid a hard veto (e.g. if one partner scores very low on Openness, avoid maximally challenging content); (3) INTERSECTION — only content predicted acceptable to both. For two people specifically, least-misery and 'fairness/turn-taking' strategies tend to outperform pure averaging because a single dissatisfied member in a dyad is highly salient (no majority to dilute one veto). Emerging work (e.g. personality-guided preference aggregators, arXiv 2304.08851) shows that incorporating personality into the aggregation step — rather than only aggregating predicted ratings — improves group outcomes, because personality predicts *how* a member compromises (an agreeable member yields, a disagreeable one does not). Concrete HelpME2C application: use the *distance* between two partners' trait vectors (esp. Openness gap) as a compatibility/risk signal, and pick an aggregation rule conditioned on that gap — average when close, least-misery when far. Direct empirical evidence that a specific strategy predicts *joint viewing satisfaction* for dyads is thin; treat the choice as a tunable, A/B-testable hyperparameter rather than a settled finding. [uncertain]
quiz_ux_implications: Suitable for direct elicitation ('rate how much you agree') — these instruments are validated as self-report Likert and do not require disguising for a low-stakes entertainment context. Biases to manage: (a) ACQUIESCENCE bias — short scales are especially vulnerable; mitigate by keeping the reverse-keyed items the TIPI/BFI-2 already include and not stripping them. (b) SOCIAL DESIRABILITY / faking — low risk here because there is no incentive to fake personality for a TV recommender (unlike recruitment, where WP29 deems such testing problematic); the stakes are recreational. (c) Low internal consistency of TIPI (alphas 0.40-0.68, Agreeableness worst) means individual scores are noisy — fine for a soft prior, not for any consequential decision. Friction-minimising format: TIPI's adjective-pair items on a single screen with a 7-point scale; consider a slider or segmented control rather than radio rows. Framing as 'help us learn your taste' (not 'a personality test') reduces both reactance and the perception of clinical assessment, which also helps the privacy posture. Avoid forced-choice ipsative formats — they break the cross-person comparability that the two-person use case depends on.
cross_cultural_validity: Mixed and a genuine risk for HelpME2C's multilingual catalog. The FFM itself replicates broadly but imperfectly across cultures, and its cross-cultural replicability is itself contested. TIPI: validated translations exist (Portuguese, Italian, plus a Frontiers 2023 scoping review cataloguing many versions/translations), but its two-item-per-domain structure *precludes formal measurement-invariance testing*, so you cannot statistically confirm a Korean TIPI score means the same as an English one — a hard limitation for any cross-language combination of profiles. BFI-2 has more language adaptations with better psychometric documentation (validated German, French BFI-2-S/XS adaptations published), and because it has more items per domain it *supports* invariance analysis where TIPI does not. BFI-S has strong German validation (SOEP) but less multilingual coverage. Known failure mode: acquiescence and extreme-response styles vary systematically by culture and contaminate Likert short forms; reverse-keyed items partially correct this. For HelpME2C specifically: if cross-language profile comparability matters (it does for joint dyads spanning languages), the BFI-2 family is the safer choice than TIPI because invariance can at least be tested; do not assume a raw TIPI Openness score is comparable across Korean/Japanese/Arabic users without validation. [uncertain]
privacy_consent_implications: Personality profiles are personal data and, under GDPR, edge toward Article 9 special-category territory because personality inferences can correlate with or be used to infer health (e.g. Neuroticism ~ mental-health proxy), beliefs, or political orientation. ICO guidance: data becomes special-category when you *intentionally draw* such an inference or treat someone differently based on it. HelpME2C's use (entertainment taste) does not intend health/belief inference, so the raw trait scores are likely ordinary personal data — but storing a Neuroticism score is a foreseeable re-identification/sensitive-inference risk and a regulator could view aggregated profiling as Article 9-adjacent. Recommendations: obtain explicit, freely-given consent at quiz time with a clear purpose statement ('to seed recommendations'); avoid solely-automated decisions with significant effect on the user (recommendations are low-stakes, so likely fine); minimise — prefer storing the derived recommendation prior over raw clinical-looking trait scores, or store transiently and discard after seeding; never label stored fields with clinical terms ('neuroticism') in user-facing surfaces. Aligns with the repo's ADR-0012 deletion rules — these scores must be deletable on DSAR and hard-deleted on account deletion. Stop-and-ask territory per CLAUDE.md §4 (privacy-control flows).
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Mapping is strongest for Openness and reasonable-but-weaker for the other four. HIGH OPENNESS -> nonlinear-storytelling, earned-ambiguity (inferred from 'ambiguity tolerance'), slow-burn, character-study, meditation-on-time, meditation-on-death, magical-realism, absurdist-comedy, satire-of-power, cosmic-horror, folk-horror, alternate-history, first-contact (novelty/complexity/aesthetic-unfamiliarity cluster — best-evidenced via Rentfrow Aesthetic+Cerebral factors). LOW OPENNESS -> conventional structure: whodunit, workplace-procedural, underdog-rise, fish-out-of-water (familiar, resolved forms). HIGH NEGATIVE EMOTIONALITY / NEUROTICISM -> existential-horror, grief-and-loss, loneliness-and-isolation, trauma-survival, mental-illness-portrayal, addiction-and-recovery (Rentfrow 'Dark' dimension; mood-congruent and mood-regulation viewing). HIGH EXTRAVERSION -> ensemble-mosaic, found-family, friendship-tested, heist-and-caper, absurdist/dark-comedy (Rentfrow 'Communal'/upbeat-social). HIGH CONSCIENTIOUSNESS -> moral structure and order: ethical-dilemma, guilt-and-atonement, redemption-arc, vigilante-justice, institutional-failure (rule/consequence themes) — weak evidence. [uncertain] LOW AGREEABLENESS -> power-corrupts, vengeance-and-its-cost, moral-compromise, betrayal-and-forgiveness, complicity-with-evil, rise-and-fall (antiheroes, conflict). HIGH AGREEABLENESS -> relationship/warmth cluster: found-family, platonic-soulmates, coming-of-age, mentor-and-pupil. Sensation-seeking (a facet correlated with low Conscientiousness + Extraversion + high Openness, not a Big Five domain itself) -> chase-and-escape, survival-and-endurance, post-apocalyptic-survival, thrilling content (Rentfrow 'Thrilling'). Most of these beyond Openness are reasoned extrapolations, not directly measured — flag as hypotheses to validate against interaction data.
implementation_recommendation: INCLUDE (partial). Recommend the TIPI (10 items) as the onboarding default IF a single-language/Western-leaning launch, OR the BFI-2-XS (15 items) if cross-language profile comparability is needed at launch (its invariance-testability is worth the 5 extra items given HelpME2C's multilingual + two-person requirements). Format: direct-elicitation Likert (7-point), one screen, framed as 'help us learn your taste', adjective-pair (TIPI) or short-statement (BFI-2-XS) items. Placement: optional/skippable step *after* account creation but *before* first recommendation, positioned as a cold-start accelerator the user can skip. Do NOT gate the product on it. Use it strictly as a soft prior that biases the initial candidate pool (esp. via Openness -> complexity/ambiguity theme weights) and decays as real interactions arrive. For the two-person core: store both partners' five-domain vectors, compute an Openness-gap compatibility signal, and select aggregation rule (average vs least-misery) as a tunable hyperparameter. DEPENDENCIES: most valuable when combined with a lightweight values/mood elicitation and the theme vocabulary — personality alone is a weak ranker. Given small effect sizes, treat the quiz as one of several cold-start seeds, not the engine. Privacy: explicit consent + minimise stored fields + deletable per ADR-0012; this touches privacy-control flows so confirm before building (CLAUDE.md §4). North-Star trace: 'a brief personality quiz seeds a same-taste prior for both partners so their very first joint recommendation is plausibly watchable before any viewing history exists' — traceable.
key_sources: Soto & John (2017), 'Short and extra-short forms of the Big Five Inventory-2: The BFI-2-S and BFI-2-XS', Journal of Research in Personality — free PDF: https://www.colby.edu/wp-content/uploads/2013/08/Soto_John_2017b.pdf (BFI-2 home + scoring: https://www.colby.edu/academics/.../the-bfi-2/). Gosling, Rentfrow & Swann (2003), TIPI — scale + scoring free: https://gosling.psy.utexas.edu/scales-weve-developed/ten-item-personality-measure-tipi/ ; TIPI scoping review (Frontiers 2023, open access): https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1202953/full . Gerlitz & Schupp (2005) / Hahn, Gosling et al. (2012) BFI-S validity — open PMC: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3098347/ ; GESIS ZIS entry (free): https://access.gesis.org/zis/1144 . Rentfrow, Goldberg & Zilca (2011), 'Listening, Watching, and Reading: The Structure and Correlates of Entertainment Preferences', Journal of Personality — open PMC: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2964424/ (canonical media-preference link). German BFI-2-S/XS validation: https://econtent.hogrefe.com/doi/abs/10.1027/1015-5759/a000481 (paywalled). Personality-guided group aggregation: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2304.08851 (free preprint). GDPR special-category guidance: ICO https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/lawful-basis/special-category-data/ (free).
